Now what happens sometimes, in fact very often, as we actually have the three tens lined up at a pretty high level, and by the way, the way you know they're lined up is by using your ears and listening for cues. Now I'm going to show you how to do that really at a high level when we get to the triggers in both the unconscious and conscious ways in which you can actually tell if you have your person at a high level of certainty. But as I was saying, very often you have someone that's feeling really certain about all three tens and they still don't buy from you. You know, like they're like, oh my god, Jordan, I love, yeah, I love the stock and wow, you're a great broker and you're company, yeah, sounds great too, but just not buying. If you've been in that situation where you had someone really, really close that just wouldn't take the plunge, well there's a reason for that. You see there's something else at work, another force, and it's your fourth element of the straight line. It's called the action threshold. And what the action threshold is, it's the level of certainty that a prospect has to be at before he or she will buy. Let me repeat that. It's the level of certainty that a prospect has to be at before he or she will buy. Let me give you an example here. Now me, I am what you call a born buyer. I buy everything. I mean, you could sell me a bridge for crying out loud if you really wanted to. I just love to buy stuff, even if it's crap. So of course I have a house full of crap I never use. I see something, I buy it. Boom, no big deal. Even if I never use it, it doesn't matter. I just don't buy stuff and I feel good about it. In fact, not that long ago, I'll tell you a story here. I was walking through Perth, Western Australia in the airport, and as I approached the gate, I heard out of the corner of the sound and I look and I see this young Asian kid and he's got this golf club in his hand. He's got some stance and promotional stance of some sort, right? And out of the corner of my eye, I watched him take another golf swing. And it was a nice golf swing. And he hit the ball and from where I was looking at the damn ball, I was looking to see who's flying over at the gate, right? And then I looked at the end of the club and sure enough, the ball was stuck to the end of the club. It turns out it was actually a training system this guy had, right? And some company at a park where they put Velcro on the face of the golf club, then they had Velcro that was covering like the soft golf ball. And when you swung, what happened was the ball would stick on the end of the club and based on where it stuck, you can tell if you were going to hook or slice the golf ball. So if you're hitting the ball on the inside, you're going to hook the ball. And if you're hitting it on the outside, you then slice the ball. So I watched this thing in soccer and that I am, I walk over and say, hey, that looks pretty cool. And I'm watching this guy and say, what is this thing? How does it work? And the kid says, oh, it's really simple watch. And he lines up his ball and he takes his stance and he hits this beautiful golf swing. And sure enough, he shows me the end and he goes, look, I hit this here right on the inside of the ball here and sure enough, because of that, I hit what's going to be a nice solid draw. Then he tells me you can also see if you're hitting the ball too close to the heel or too close to the toe. So you're not getting a solid shot. So I thought about that for a second and I was like, well, how much does that cost? And he goes, oh, it's only $49. I'm like, really? I'll take it. And I bought it just right there on the spot. And just so you know, I knew the thing was basically a piece of garbage. I'm like, there's no way this thing is actually going to really work. I'll probably use it once and then never use it again because it's a stupid thing. But I bought it anyway. I'm not a stupid guy, just so you know. I actually, and listen, this is important here, I actually ran a buying strategy through my mind. It was a conscious decision I made and it happened just like that. You see, the way we actually make decisions, all of us, is we run these parallel movies for our mind. Before we buy, you'll project down the road in an instant and you'll say to yourself, if I buy this, am I going to feel more pain or more pleasure? And likewise, if I don't buy it, will I feel more pain or more pleasure? In essence, your brain is running these movies and it's happening so fast that you don't even realize it. But it actually sized the whole thing up in my mind. I was like, how much does it cost? And he repeated his number, $49. And I asked myself this magic question that every person asks themselves before they make any buying decision. And the question is this. What's the worst that can possibly happen? What's the worst that can possibly happen? Every human being, before they make any decision, they always look at the downside before they buy. They say, what's the worst that can possibly happen? Let's say this kid was a total scam artist. The thing was a total piece of crap and, you know, totally useless. What's the worst that's going to happen? Is that going to put me in the poor house, $49? No. Am I going to feel like an idiot about it? Not really because it's only $49. So what the hell? But if it works the way he says, on the other hand, let's just talk about the upside here. Well, damn, I've been having problems with my golf swing shank in the ball for as long as I can remember. And I'd be able to get rid of that shank once and for all to be able to hit that beautiful drive like this young Asian kid. And I feel so good when I'm sitting in the clubhouse with my buddies after a long round of golf, having a beer, totally new and improved golf swing. You see what's happening here? I'm projecting. I'm playing the movie out here for you in the future. It's what we call future pacing and decision. I actually future pace my decision and based on the outcomes I get, I'll either buy or not buy. See, if my movie tells me they're going to have a lot of pleasure if I buy and not that much pain, then I'll buy. And if it's the other way around, then guess what? I won't buy. So when I asked the guy how much is it costing, he said $49. I said to myself, what's the worst that could possibly happen here? Is it going to destroy me financially? Is it going to put me in the poor house? Okay, now logically, let's go to that. That's emotion. Now, logically, it's myself. All right, it's not that expensive. So it does sort of make logical sense, this device, meaning on the physics side, I kind of ran the physics through my mind. It wasn't that stupid, right? Since I was able to justify it logically, I was then free to be moved emotionally, which was actually seeing myself on the course with the beautiful swing and feeling good sitting in the clubhouse and imagining I'm on the 15th hole of Pebble Beach and not hitting me into the water the way I did last time. That's a true story, by the way. Seriously, I played Pebble Beach and it was one of the worst, most awful rounds of my life. And in consequence, I felt ultimate pain from that. So when I bought this crappy product on pure emotion, I imagined seeing myself on the 15th tee of August, excuse me, of Pebble Beach and feeling great. Okay, and that's me. Basically, I have what's called a low action threshold. And because of that, you can basically sell me anything whether I need it or not, as long as I don't think the thing will kill me. Now let me give you the polar opposite of myself, someone with an ultra high action threshold. My father, whose name is Max, and if you read the book, his name is Mad Max. Well, my dad, he is literally the toughest buyer on the planet. He doesn't buy anything unless he's absolutely certain and damn sure of it. He's not buying any miracle golf kills. He's not walking through any airports and buying anything. He'd be like that bastard, who is that guy with his golf equipment, his golf swing, what is that all about? What is that bastard think he is? He was always calling everyone bastards, my dad. Even though he never met before, the bastard is the IRS, the bastard is the Ford Motor Company, you put a rattle on his car. Let's just say he was not an open guy when it came to getting sold stuff. Some people would just like that. They're just tough cells and it's called having a high action threshold. Now watch the graph here. Here's how this plays out. People with high action thresholds. Now right here, this is a one to ten in a scale of certainty. Now someone with a high action threshold, they got to go all the way here, boom. They got to be at a ten, ten, ten and damn sure of it before they buy anything. You get it? You have to make them absolutely, positively certain if you want to close them on anything. That means number one, they have to be sure as hell if the practice is going to be great and then it satisfies both their logical and their emotional needs, all of them. Two, they got to be sure you're an honest salesperson, ethical and you're not going to be ripping them off in any way whatsoever. And three, that your company itself is going to stand behind the product for the future. They've got to be at a ten, ten, ten and damn sure of it. If they're at an eight, eight, eight, which is kind of like right over here on the graph, will they buy? Yes or no? The answer is no. They will not buy at an eight, ten, eight, eight, nine, eight, they'll only buy when you get into a ten, ten, ten, which is very often you're in that situation where you're like, jeez, I don't get it. They seem like they really love my product and they kind of like me too, yet they just still said they wanted to think about it. The reason was because they had a very high action threshold. So what we do with the straight line is we actually lower their action threshold temporarily at just the right moment. You see, you can't lower someone's action threshold forever. It's only for the moment and it's only for a fleeting moment at that, maybe five or ten minutes at most. So you have to know exactly when to do it, which is only after you've already established those three tens at a very, very high level. Then you'll lower the action threshold, which is your fourth key element to the straight line. Now, let me explain exactly what a person's action threshold is, what it's made up of, what it is basically is the sum of all their values and beliefs about buying and making decisions and how they perceive the world essentially. Some people have a belief that the only good decision I make is one that takes me three weeks to make. I want my decision slowly and other people say, I go out my gut. I see it, I like it, boom, I buy it. Now what you're looking to do as a salesperson is you're looking to actually get someone's level of certainty above their action threshold. Yet if you're having problems doing that, which sometimes happens, then you actually go the other way. You get certainty as much as possible and then you lower the action threshold afterwards so it drops below certainty and then boom, people start to buy.
